title: AT — Specific Times
definition: Use AT for precise clock times and specific moments
example1: The meeting starts at 3 PM.
example2: I wake up at 7:00 every morning.
example3: She left at midnight.
usage1: When the answer is an exact hour or a specific point in time
tip: Think of AT as pointing to a dot on a timeline — one exact moment

title: AT — Holidays & Festivals (without "day")
definition: Use AT for holidays when you don't say the word "Day"
example1: We exchange gifts at Christmas.
example3: They always travel at New Year.
usage1: For celebrations and festival periods
tip: But say ON Christmas Day, ON Easter Sunday

title: AT — Night
definition: Use AT with "night" (exception to morning/afternoon/evening)
example1: I can't sleep at night.
example3: The store closes at night.
usage1: Referring to nighttime hours
tip: We say IN the morning/afternoon/evening, but always AT night

title: AT — Weekend (British vs American)
definition: British English uses AT the weekend; American uses ON the weekend
example1: What are you doing at the weekend? (UK)
example2: I'll call you at the weekend. (UK)
usage1: Talking about Saturday and Sunday
tip: Americans say "ON the weekend" — both are correct for your dialect

title: IN — Months & Years
definition: Use IN for months, years, decades, centuries
example1: My birthday is in May.
example3: The war ended in the 20th century.
usage1: When the time unit is larger than a day
tip: IN = inside a larger time container

title: IN — Seasons
definition: Use IN with seasons (spring, summer, autumn/fall, winter)
example1: Leaves change color in autumn.
example2: We go to the beach in summer.
example3: Flowers bloom in spring.
usage1: Describing when something happens during a season
tip: Exception: "ON a spring morning" when you add a specific day part

title: IN — Parts of the Day (morning/afternoon/evening)
definition: Use IN with morning, afternoon, and evening
example1: I drink coffee in the morning.
example3: She studies in the evening.
usage1: General times during the day (but not night)
tip: Exception: AT night, and ON Monday morning (when adding a day)

title: IN — Duration (time to complete something)
definition: Use IN to mean "after this amount of time" or "within this period"
example1: I can run 5 km in 20 minutes.
example2: She finished the test in one hour.
example3: The pizza will be ready in 10 minutes.
usage1: Talking about how long something takes
tip: For future: "I'll be there IN 5 minutes" = 5 minutes from now

title: ON — Days & Dates
definition: Use ON for specific days and calendar dates
example1: Let's meet on Friday.
example3: I have a doctor's appointment on Wednesday.
usage1: Any named day (Monday, etc.) or date (May 5th)
tip: ON = sitting on top of that specific day on the calendar

title: ON — Special Named Days (with "Day")
definition: Use ON for holidays that include the word "Day"
example1: We open presents on Christmas Day.
example2: Restaurants are busy on Mother's Day.
example3: I'll call you on New Year's Day.
usage1: Christmas Day, New Year's Day, Mother's Day, etc.
tip: Compare: AT Christmas (season) vs ON Christmas Day (specific day)

title: ON — Weekend (American English)
definition: Americans use ON the weekend
example1: I relax on the weekend.
example2: What did you do on the weekend?
example3: We often travel on the weekend.
usage1: Same meaning as AT the weekend (British)
tip: Both ON and AT are correct depending on your dialect

title: ON — Day + Part of Day
definition: Use ON when you combine a day with morning/afternoon/evening
example1: I'll see you on Monday morning.
example3: We arrived on Sunday evening.
usage1: To specify exactly when something happens
tip: Without the day, use IN (in the morning). With the day, use ON

title: ON — My Birthday / That Day
definition: Use ON for "my birthday" and phrases like "that day"
example1: I was born on my birthday.
example3: On the first day of school...
usage1: Referring to specific personal dates
tip: Any specific named day = ON

title: No Preposition — Last / Next / This / Every
definition: Do NOT use at/in/on with last, next, this, every, tomorrow, yesterday
example1: I'll see you next Friday. (not on next Friday)
example3: We met last year. (not on last year)
usage1: These words already include time meaning
tip: If you see last/next/this/every → drop the preposition completely

title: AT vs IN vs ON — Time Summary
definition: AT = precise time, ON = specific day, IN = longer period
example1: AT 5 PM / AT midnight / AT lunchtime
example2: ON Monday / ON May 5th / ON Christmas Day
example3: IN May / IN 2024 / IN summer / IN the morning
usage1: Quick decision guide
tip: AT is a point, ON is a day, IN is a container

title: AT or IN or ON — Meeting Time
explanation: AT is used for specific clock times.

title: ON or IN — Day
explanation: ON is used for days of the week.

title: Christmas Day vs Christmas Season
explanation: ON is used when "Day" is included.

title: Christmas Season
explanation: AT is used for holidays/season without "Day".

title: Summer
question_text: We go to the beach ______ summer.

title: Weekend (US)
question_text: What are you doing ______ the weekend? (American English)

title: Weekend (UK)
question_text: What are you doing ______ the weekend? (British English)
explanation: British English uses AT the weekend.

title: Duration
explanation: IN = after this amount of time / within.

title: AT — Specific Points & Locations
definition: Use AT for specific points, addresses, or exact locations
example1: Meet me at the bus stop.
example3: Turn left at the traffic light.
usage1: When thinking of a place as a point on a map
tip: AT = a precise point, not an area

title: AT — Events & Gatherings
definition: Use AT for events, concerts, parties, meetings
example1: I saw her at the concert.
example3: He spoke at the conference.
usage1: When you're attending something
tip: AT the cinema/theater/museum (as an event destination)

title: AT — Top / Bottom / End of something
definition: Use AT for extremities of objects
example1: Write your name at the top of the page.
example2: There's a signature at the bottom.
example3: My house is at the end of the street.
usage1: Position at an edge or limit
tip: AT = a terminal point

title: IN — Enclosed Spaces
definition: Use IN for spaces with boundaries (rooms, boxes, buildings, countries)
example1: The keys are in my bag.
example3: I live in Brazil.
usage1: When something is inside a 3D space
tip: IN = inside something with walls or borders

title: IN — Water bodies
definition: Use IN for oceans, seas, rivers, lakes, pools
example1: She swam in the ocean.
example3: Fish live in the river.
usage1: Being submerged or inside the water
tip: ON the water = on the surface (boat). IN = inside/immersed.

title: IN — Cities & Neighborhoods
definition: Use IN for cities, towns, neighborhoods, districts
example1: She lives in Tokyo.
example2: There's a café in my neighborhood.
example3: I work in Manhattan.
usage1: A larger area that contains you
tip: But say AT an address (point) vs IN a city (area)

title: ON — Surfaces
definition: Use ON for surfaces (tables, floors, walls, ceilings)
example1: The book is on the table.
example3: There's a painting on the wall.
usage1: Something touching a flat surface
tip: ON = in contact with a surface (not inside)

title: ON — Streets & Avenues
definition: Use ON for street names (American English)
example1: The store is on Main Street.
example3: Turn left on 5th Avenue.
usage1: Describing location along a road
tip: British English sometimes uses IN + street; American uses ON

title: ON — Public Transport
definition: Use ON for buses, trains, planes, ships
example1: She's on the bus.
example2: I met her on the train.
example3: We ate lunch on the plane.
usage1: When you board a vehicle you can walk inside
tip: But IN a car / IN a taxi (smaller vehicles)

title: ON — Body parts (not enclosed)
definition: Use ON for body parts (if not inside)
example1: She has a ring on her finger.
example2: He kissed me on the cheek.
example3: I got a mosquito bite on my arm.
usage1: Location on the surface of the body
tip: But something IN your mouth, IN your eye

title: IN vs ON — Car vs Bus
definition: Use IN for cars/taxis, ON for buses/trains/planes
example1: We drove in her car.
example3: She was in the taxi.
usage1: Size and ability to stand up determines the preposition
tip: IN = small/private, ON = large/public

title: AT vs IN — Work / School
definition: AT work, AT school, AT home (general location); IN the office, IN the classroom (specific room)
example1: She's at work right now.
example3: The students are at school.
usage1: AT = the institution; IN = the physical space
tip: AT = activity/function, IN = physical container

title: ON — Floor of a building
definition: Use ON with floor numbers
example1: My apartment is on the 3rd floor.
example2: The cafeteria is on the ground floor.
example3: She lives on the top floor.
usage1: Specifying which level
tip: ON the floor = you're on that surface/level

title: AT — Home / Work / School
definition: Use AT with home, work, school (no article)
example1: I'm at home.
example3: The kids are at school.
usage1: Common fixed phrases
tip: Never say "AT the home" — drop the article for these phrases

title: ON — Left / Right
definition: Use ON for left and right sides
example1: The bank is on the left.
example3: She sits on my left.
usage1: Indicating direction or position
tip: ON the left/right = position relative to something

title: TO — Direction / Movement
definition: Use TO to show movement toward a destination
example1: I'm going to the store.
example3: Send this email to your boss.
usage1: When something or someone is going somewhere
tip: TO = toward, in the direction of

title: TO — Receiver
definition: Use TO for the person/thing that receives an action or object
example1: I gave the book to Maria.
example2: Can you explain this to me?
example3: She showed her ID to the guard.
usage1: Give, send, tell, show, explain + TO someone
tip: After verbs of giving/communicating → use TO

title: TO — Comparison / Ratio
definition: Use TO in comparisons (similar to, equal to, compared to)
example1: This is similar to that.
example3: I prefer tea to coffee.
usage1: Showing relationship between two things
tip: "Prefer X TO Y" is correct

title: TO — Time (until)
definition: Use TO when telling time before the hour (US: OF)
example1: It's ten to three. (2:50)
example3: Five minutes to midnight.
usage1: Minutes to the next hour
tip: British: TO, American also uses OF (ten of three)

title: FOR — Purpose / Function
definition: Use FOR to explain the purpose of an object or action
example1: This knife is for cutting bread.
example2: I use this app for learning English.
example3: Let's go out for dinner.
usage1: What is it used for?
tip: FOR = intended purpose. "Use X for Y."

title: FOR — Benefit
definition: Use FOR to show who benefits or receives something good
example1: I made this cake for you.
example2: Can you carry this for me?
example3: She works hard for her family.
usage1: Doing something to help someone
tip: FOR = on behalf of / to help

title: FOR — Duration
definition: Use FOR with a period of time (how long)
example1: I lived there for three years.
example3: Wait for five minutes.
usage1: Duration of an action or state
tip: FOR + length of time (SINCE + specific start time)

title: FOR — Reason / Cause
definition: Use FOR to give a reason (similar to "because of")
example1: This city is famous for its food.
example2: She was punished for being late.
example3: Thank you for your help.
usage1: Explaining why something happened
tip: FOR = due to / because of

title: FOR — Exchange / Price
definition: Use FOR with prices and exchanges
example1: I bought this for $20.
example2: She traded her car for a bike.
example3: You get a discount for paying cash.
usage1: What you pay in exchange for something
tip: Pay + money + FOR something

title: TO vs FOR — Destination vs Purpose (Travel)
definition: Use TO for destination (going to a place), FOR for purpose of trip
example1: I'm flying to London. (destination)
example2: I'm flying to London for work. (both)
example3: She came to the party for the food.
usage1: I went TO Paris (destination) vs I went FOR a meeting (purpose)
tip: TO = where? / FOR = why?

title: TO vs FOR — Benefit
question_text: I made this cake ______ my sister.

title: TO vs FOR — Receiver vs Benefit
question_text: I gave the book ______ John. vs I bought the book ______ John.

title: Mixed TO/FOR Review
opt1: I went to the store for buy milk.
opt2: I went to the store to buy milk.
opt3: I went for the store to buy milk.
opt4: I went for the store for buy milk.
explanation: TO + verb (purpose) or FOR + noun. "To buy" is correct.
